did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically transfer learning with the AlexNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), to classify skin lesions (melanoma vs. nevus) using datasets such as HAM10000, MSK, and UDA. It compares full and partial fine-tuning strategies to obtain classification accuracy results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing full and partial fine-tuning of the AlexNet model for skin lesion classification. It details specific methodologies (transfer learning, fine-tuning strategies), datasets used (HAM10000, MSK, UDA), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy percentages and training times). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICITEE), it contains original research data and analysis rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: The Effect of Partial Fine Tuning on AlexNet for Skin Lesions Classification
journal: 2021 13th International Conference on Information Technology and Electrical Engineering (ICITEE)
brief_summary: This study investigates the effect of partial fine-tuning on the AlexNet Convolutional Neural Network for classifying skin lesions into melanoma and nevus. The researchers compared full fine-tuning (all 25 layers) against partial fine-tuning (layers 13 to 25) using three datasets: HAM10000, MSK, and UDA, each containing 400 images per class. Evaluation metrics included classification accuracy and training time, with models tested on 200 images per category from each dataset. The partial fine-tuning model achieved slightly higher accuracies (92.25%, 77.75%, 85.75%) compared to full fine-tuning (91%, 75.75%, 84.75%) while significantly reducing training time from 82 minutes to 63 minutes. The results indicate that partial fine-tuning provides a negligible improvement in accuracy but offers a substantial advantage in training efficiency for this specific architecture and task.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as Class 1 in the binary classification task. The paper notes it is the deadliest form of skin cancer and forms malignant tumors in the basal layer of the epidermis.
Classified as Class 2 in the binary classification task. Described as a common benign neoplasm in pigment cells, also known as a mole, originating from melanocytes.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and nevus classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper implements transfer learning on AlexNet to classify skin lesions into two discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma (Class 1) and nevus (Class 2). The model uses a modified final layer (fc8) with 2 neurons and a softmax classifier to predict these classes. Evaluation metrics include classification accuracy, recall, and specificity across three datasets (HAM10000, MSK, UDA).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of common pigmented skin lesions from the Medical University of Vienna (ViDIR Dataverse). A subset of 1100 images per class was used in this study.

use.training: Used for training the AlexNet model (800 images per class).
use.validation: Used for validation to determine optimal learning rate and epochs (100 images per class).
use.testing: Used for intra-dataset testing (200 images per class).

datasets_used.name: MSK
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing RGB skin dermoscopic images from the Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center, obtained from various international hospitals.

use.testing: Used for inter-dataset testing (200 images per class).

datasets_used.name: UDA
datasets_used.description: A sub-dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC).

use.testing: Used for inter-dataset testing (200 images per class).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the classification accuracy across three different datasets (HAM10000, MSK, and UDA) for both full and partial fine-tuning models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared full and partial fine-tuning using classification accuracy and training time, describing the accuracy improvement as 'insignificant' and the training time reduction as 'quite significant' based on descriptive statistics (mean accuracy and standard deviation) rather than inferential statistical tests.

HAM10000.notes: Both models achieved performance metrics above 90%. The difference in performance between full and partial fine-tuning was not significant.

MSK.notes: Accuracy dropped compared to HAM10000. Models produced more false positives than false negatives. Precision was in the range of 50-60%. The difference in performance between full and partial fine-tuning was not significant.

UDA.notes: Models identified the negative class (nevus) very well (almost 100%), but misidentified the positive class (melanoma) significantly. This may be due to heavy vignetting in UDA melanoma images. The difference in performance between full and partial fine-tuning was not significant.

Aggregate_Results.description: Mean and Standard Deviation of accuracy across HAM10000, MSK, and UDA
Aggregate_Results.notes: Partial fine-tuning showed a negligible improvement in accuracy but significantly reduced training time (63 minutes 10 seconds) compared to full fine-tuning (82 minutes 43 seconds).

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (1.2 million images, 1,000 categories) for the International Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) 2012

architecture_details.base_layers: 22 layers (convolutional and pooling layers) kept frozen in partial fine-tuning, unfrozen in full fine-tuning
architecture_details.layer_composition: Input/output layers, 7 ReLU activation layers, 2 normalization layers, 3 pooling layers, 2 dropout layers, 8 learnable weight layers (5 convolutional, 3 fully connected)
architecture_details.modified_layers: Original fc8 (1000 neurons) replaced with new fc8 (2 neurons for binary classification), new softmax layer, and cross-entropy loss function

training_details.hardware: GPU (specific model not reported, noted to work on limited GPUs)
training_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Compared full fine-tuning (all 25 layers) vs partial fine-tuning (layers 13 to 25 unfrozen, layers 1-12 frozen)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing input images to 227x227x3 to fit the AlexNet input size
conclusions: Partial fine-tuning (layers 13 to 25) of the AlexNet model provides an insignificant improvement in classification accuracy compared to full fine-tuning (all 25 layers) for skin lesion classification.
Partial fine-tuning significantly reduces training time (63 minutes 10 seconds) compared to full fine-tuning (82 minutes 43 seconds), offering a substantial advantage in training efficiency.
Both full and partial fine-tuning models achieved high accuracy (>90%) on the intra-dataset test using HAM10000, with no significant performance difference between the two methods.

Performance dropped significantly on inter-dataset tests (MSK and UDA), indicating challenges in generalization due to differences in dataset characteristics such as background color and image vignetting.
The models produced more false positives than false negatives on inter-dataset tests, resulting in low precision (50-60%) for melanoma detection.
Direct comparison with previous research is difficult due to the lack of inter-dataset testing in prior studies, though the proposed system's results are comparable to existing literature.

Future work should include image segmentation before classification to improve performance on datasets with varying characteristics and investigate the effect of different learning rate values on model performance.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (melanoma vs. nevus) using only the AlexNet architecture, restricting generalizability to other lesion types or modern architectures.

Performance drops significantly on inter-dataset testing (MSK and UDA) compared to intra-dataset testing (HAM10000), indicating poor generalization and sensitivity to dataset-specific biases such as background color (beige vs. magenta) and image vignetting.
The models exhibited low precision (50-60%) on inter-dataset tests due to a high rate of false positives, which is a critical clinical concern for melanoma detection.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the accuracy differences; it relies on descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) to claim 'insignificant' accuracy improvements.
Direct comparison with prior literature is limited because previous studies largely lacked inter-dataset testing, making it difficult to assess true external validity.
The dataset sizes used for testing were small (200 images per class per dataset), which may limit the statistical power of the evaluation.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may have contributed to the observed performance gaps and potential overfitting on specific dataset characteristics.
The study acknowledges that the UDA dataset contains heavy vignetting in melanoma images, which likely skewed performance metrics and highlights a dependency on image quality/standardization.
